title: Plastic waste generation
subtitle: This measures total plastic waste generation prior to management and therefore does not represent the quantity of plastic at risk of polluting waterways, rivers and the ocean environment.
caption:
Plastic waste generation, 2010

This measures total plastic waste generation prior to management and therefore does not represent the quantity of plastic at risk of polluting waterways, rivers and the ocean environment.

This choropleth map displays the total plastic waste generation for countries worldwide in 2010, measured in metric tons. The visualization uses a sequential color scale ranging from light beige to deep maroon to represent quantities from 0 tons up to 10 million tons. The highest values, indicated by the darkest maroon shading, are concentrated in a small number of nations. The United States and China both appear in the highest band, exceeding 10 million tons of plastic waste generation. Brazil also falls within this top tier, showing a similarly dark shade. Moving down the scale, countries shaded in medium orange tones represent generation levels between 2.5 million and 7.5 million tons; this category includes India, Russia, Germany, and the United Kingdom. Canada, Mexico, and several nations in Western Europe display lighter orange hues, corresponding to the 1 million to 2.5 million ton range. Large portions of the African continent, along with countries in Central Asia and parts of South America, are shaded in the lightest beige, indicating generation levels below 1 million tons. Several regions, including Greenland, Western Sahara, and French Guiana, are marked with diagonal hatching to signify that no data is available for these areas. The distribution shows a heavy concentration of waste generation in the world's largest economies and most populous nations, while vast geographic areas contribute significantly lower volumes. The data highlights a disparity where a few specific countries generate volumes an order of magnitude higher than the global median.
